rationale: Acid rock drainage, sediment discharge, tailings seepage are mining's largest water-pollution footprint.
Acids/solvents in hydrometallurgy and SX-EW; effluent contamination.
Wet scrubber wastewater, stormwater runoff from slag.
Leaching chemicals, wash water; risk of closed-loop failure.
Transport spills at ports and in transit; bunker contamination.
Dewatering of pit, large process water demand, often in arid basins.
Wash and separation water, lower than virgin processing.
Primary and only significant source; TSFs store mineral waste.
Residues (e.g., red mud from alumina) are similar hazards.
Spent acids, solvents, filter cakes; hazardous streams.
Slag, flue dust, sludge, spent refractories.
Hazardous secondary waste streams, highly variable quality.
Habitat destruction, fragmentation, species loss from open-pit footprint.
Facility footprint; localized pollution impacts on species.
Acid rain and heavy metal deposition can eliminate sensitive taxa.
Shipping lanes, port disturbance, oil spills on marine species.
Land conversion; wastewater impacts on aquatic ecosystems.
SO2 acidification of surrounding ecosystems (classic smelter die-off).
Blasting, haul trucks, drills, crushers routinely exceed 100 dBA.
Furnace fans, oxygen lances, slag handling.
Shredders and balers produce high dBA levels.
Fugitive dust (PM10), diesel exhaust from haul trucks.
Stack emissions of metal fumes and volatile organic compounds.
SO2, NOx, PM, heavy metal fumes (As, Pb, Cd, Hg).
Shipping bunker fuel combustion; port emissions.
Metal deposition in 10 km radius documented globally.
Iron/steel and aluminum smelting are emissions-intensive globally.
Silicosis, injury, noise-induced hearing loss, community exposure.

Hazardous exposure, especially in informal recycling.
